nativeRange.habitat: Freshwater lakes situated at the headwaters of the Ajamaru River; inhabits Lake Aitinjo and the Ajamaru Lakes system in the Irian Jaya region of West Papua

decorPreferences: Dense planting at sides and back for cover and egg-laying sites
Spawning mop or fine-leaved plants for breeding

diet.notes: Generally takes a wide variety of foods including quality flakes, small pellets, and frozen or freeze-dried fare such as bloodworms, brine shrimp, and daphnia. Including vegetable-based foods alongside protein-rich items tends to support good health and color.

summary: Iconic two-tone rainbowfish with a vivid blue-violet head and orange-yellow rear body; males are far more colorful than females. Critically Endangered in the wild — only captive-bred stock should be purchased.
careNotes: IUCN ENDANGERED in the wild due to over-collection and habitat loss restricted to the Ajamaru Lakes system in West Papua, Indonesia. Only captive-bred specimens should be purchased; wild-caught collection is considered unethical and captive-bred stock is widely available. Best kept in groups of 6 or more, with a ratio of 2–3 females per male — males display their best colors when competing for females' attention. Males generally reach around 10 cm; females are somewhat smaller at around 7–8 cm. Juveniles tend to look washed-out and may take up to a year to develop full adult coloration. Prefers moderately hard, slightly alkaline water consistent with their native lake environment. Lifespan is generally reported as 5–8 years under typical aquarium conditions, though some hobbyists report individuals living considerably longer. Higher water temperatures tend to shorten lifespan. An active, fast swimmer requiring a tank of at least 120 cm in length with ample open swimming space in the center and planting at the margins. Not aggressive toward tankmates, though may eat dwarf shrimp or very small fry. Does not show noticeable escape tendencies when kept in a covered aquarium.
breedingNotes: Generally considered one of the more approachable rainbowfish to spawn. Egg-scatterers that deposit adhesive eggs among fine-leaved plants or spawning mops over an extended period. Fry are small and require infusoria or commercial fry foods initially; raising fry to adulthood is commonly described as more challenging than the initial spawning itself. Separating eggs or using a dedicated breeding tank tends to improve fry survival.

primary.name: Aquarium Co-Op
primary.notes: Primary source for care parameters, tank sizing, diet, compatibility, and breeding overview.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked scientific name, family/order, native range (Ajamaru Lakes / Lake Aitinjo, Vogelkop Peninsula, West Papua), adult size (males 9.0 cm SL, females 7.0 cm SL), temperature (27–30°C), pH (7.0–8.0), and hardness (9–19 dH). Temperature max reconciled to 29°C by averaging Co-Op (28°C) and FishBase (30°C) maxima. Size reconciled to 7–10 cm range covering both sexes and both sources. IUCN Endangered status confirmed.

fish.breedingNotes: Egg-scatterer; deposits adhesive eggs onto fine-leaved plants or spawning mops. Spawning itself is relatively straightforward but raising fry is more demanding due to their small initial size.
